title: PayPal OAuth2 keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: PayPal OAuth2 client ID detected in source code. Client IDs are used to identify applications in PayPal's REST API. While not as sensitive as client secrets, exposing these in source code can reveal application architecture and enable attackers to target your PayPal integration. Store in configuration files or environment variables.

PayPal OAuth2 client secret detected in source code. Client secrets are credentials used by applications to authenticate with PayPal's OAuth2 REST API endpoints. Exposing these allows attackers to access PayPal accounts, process payments, refunds, and access sensitive transaction data. Use secure credential storage such as environment variables or secret management systems. Rotate immediately if exposed.

PayPal OAuth2 access token detected in source code. Access tokens provide direct API access to PayPal accounts and transaction data. Exposing these tokens allows attackers to make payments, process refunds, access customer payment information, and perform unauthorized transactions. These tokens typically expire but can cause significant damage while valid. Store securely and never commit to source code.

PayPal OAuth2 access token detected in Authorization header. Bearer tokens in authorization headers provide direct API access to PayPal payment services. Exposing these allows attackers to authenticate as your application, access payment data, process transactions, and potentially commit fraud. Never commit authorization headers with real tokens to source code.
